title: Eduversal Specialist Competency Content — Hand-Authored v1.0
description: Replaces the 72 algorithmically-generated entries in competency_framework/specialists/levels with 96 hand-authored Indonesia-contextual entries (24 competencies × 4 levels). Closes the depth-asymmetry gap identified in the 2026-05-08 cross-module audit (specialist track had 0% Indonesia-context density vs 1-2 for hand-authored TH/AH tracks). Brings specialist track to format-fidelity parity with teachers + leaders tracks.
format_fidelity_notes: Each entry mirrors the shape of the existing hand-authored TH/AH content: ~1000-1300 char Indonesia-contextual reading + 3 keyTakeaways + 5 selfAssessment statements + 1 activity (task / output / duration / evidence). Voice progression: awareness = 'beginning to understand', practitioner = 'consistently apply', advanced = 'systematically integrate / lead within own work', lead = 'shape network practice / coach others'. Indonesia context references partner-school cities (Jakarta, Bandung, Surabaya, Yogyakarta, Bali, Medan, Solo, Tangerang, Semarang, Malang, Makassar) + Cambridge subject codes (0610, 0580, 0500, 0625, 0654 etc.) + Permendiknas + Kemendikbudristek where relevant.
audience: subject_specialist (HQ) — gated by ch_subjects[]; the 7 active HQ Subject Specialists across the Eduversal network (Math, English, Bahasa, Physics, Chemistry, Biology, Religion).

csm-1_awareness.reading: At the Awareness level, HQ Subject Specialists are beginning to read Cambridge mark schemes the way a principal examiner does — tracing each mark to its assessment objective and explaining what students must show to earn it. A specialist at this stage can locate a mark scheme, identify the AO codes assigned to each marking point, and explain the difference between a mark for knowledge recall and a mark for analytical reasoning. For example, a newly appointed Mathematics specialist in the Eduversal HQ team reads the Cambridge IGCSE Mathematics (0580) Paper 4 mark scheme alongside the question paper and notices that some marks are awarded for method even when the final answer is wrong — a distinction many partner-school Mathematics teachers have not internalised. This practice aligns with Cambridge International Teacher Standard 3.2 (deep subject knowledge) and 3.7 (assessment expertise), and supports the Specialist's role in school-appraisal visits where mark-scheme literacy of partner teachers is part of the d2 (Teaching Quality) evidence. At this stage, the specialist is not yet running mark-scheme literacy workshops for partner schools, but is building the mark-scheme reading habit and noticing where partner teachers' mark-scheme literacy is weak.
keyTakeaways: Read mark schemes alongside question papers — annotate which marks reward knowledge vs reasoning vs application.
Notice the AO code on each marking point — it tells you what cognitive demand the question is testing.
Build a personal library of mark-scheme observations: questions where partner teachers consistently misread the marking intent.
selfAssessment: I can locate the most recent mark scheme for any paper in my Cambridge subject.
I can explain to a partner teacher how marks are distributed across AOs in a specific paper.
I notice when partner-school teachers misread mark scheme conventions (e.g., method marks, ECF, follow-through).
I can read a question paper and predict which sub-questions will be hardest based on mark-scheme weighting.
I have annotated at least three mark schemes in detail this academic year.

activity.task: Choose one Cambridge paper in your subject (most recent series). Annotate the entire mark scheme: tag each marking point with its AO code and classify each as recall / application / analysis / evaluation. Identify three marking points where partner-school teachers in your network typically misread the marking intent.
activity.output: An annotated mark scheme PDF + three observations on common partner-teacher misreadings.
activity.evidence: Upload the annotated PDF and observation notes to your IPDL.

csm-1_practitioner.reading: At the Practitioner level, HQ Subject Specialists consistently apply mark-scheme literacy as a teaching tool — not just a reading habit. A specialist at this stage uses mark schemes in coaching conversations with partner teachers, in workshop design, and in school-appraisal feedback. For example, a Biology specialist visiting a partner school in Surabaya observes a Year 11 lesson where the teacher asks students to 'describe' photosynthesis, but the next IGCSE Biology (0610) paper has marking points awarded for 'explain' (Level 2) and 'compare' (Level 3) responses — the lesson is teaching to a lower cognitive demand than the assessment requires. The specialist surfaces this gap in post-observation feedback, citing specific mark-scheme evidence. This practice aligns with CTS 3.2 + 3.7 and the Specialist's school_appraisals_v2 d2-a4 evidence (constructive feedback to teachers). At this stage, the specialist applies mark-scheme literacy fluently in feedback but does not yet design network-wide mark-scheme literacy interventions or coach other specialists.
keyTakeaways: Use mark-scheme evidence in post-observation feedback — name the AO + the marking convention specifically.
When a lesson teaches to a lower cognitive demand than the assessment requires, that is a structural teaching gap worth surfacing.
Build mark-scheme literacy modules into your subject CPD workshops — teachers need this and rarely get it elsewhere.
selfAssessment: I cite specific mark-scheme evidence in post-observation feedback at least monthly.
I have surfaced at least three teach-vs-assess gaps in partner-school visits this term.
I have run at least one mark-scheme literacy session in a partner-school CPD this year.
I can explain ECF (error carried forward) and method-mark conventions to a partner teacher with worked examples.
I notice when a partner teacher's lesson plan undershoots the assessment cognitive demand.

activity.task: During your next school-appraisal visit, conduct a paired observation focused on cognitive-demand alignment: compare the lesson's stated objective against the AO requirement of the next assessment in the same scheme of work. Document any gap. Provide post-observation feedback citing specific mark-scheme evidence.
activity.output: Paired observation note + post-observation feedback log + teacher's response.
activity.evidence: Upload the artefacts to your IPDL and discuss with your Director-mentor.

csm-1_advanced.reading: At the Advanced level, HQ Subject Specialists systematically integrate mark-scheme literacy across the network — designing partner-school teacher development around it, embedding it in Eduversal scheme-of-work standards, and using mark-scheme analysis to drive cross-school benchmarking. A specialist at this stage runs network-wide mark-scheme deep-dive sessions, contributes to the Eduversal subject pacing standards, and tracks year-on-year improvement in partner-school mark-scheme alignment. For example, an English Language specialist analyses three years of network IGCSE First Language English (0500) results across all 16 partner schools, identifies that partner teachers consistently underweight the directed-writing AO compared to its mark allocation, and designs a year-long intervention — directed-writing CPD module + scheme-of-work template revisions + cross-school exemplar lesson library. Network IGCSE English directed-writing scores improve by 8 percentile points the following year. This practice aligns with CTS 3.7 + 4.5 + Eduversal Network Standards. At this stage, the specialist designs interventions and shapes network practice, but does not yet co-author Cambridge-facing publications or examiner-training contributions.
keyTakeaways: Move from individual coaching to network-level intervention — systematic underweighting of an AO is a curriculum problem, not a teacher problem.
Use multi-year results data to identify mark-scheme alignment gaps — single-year fluctuations are noise, three-year trends are signals.
Track intervention impact: design with a measurable outcome target and verify year-on-year.
selfAssessment: I have designed at least one network-wide intervention based on mark-scheme alignment gaps this year.
I track network-wide partner-school performance against AO-by-AO mark distribution.
I contribute to Eduversal subject pacing standards with mark-scheme rationale.
I can describe one specific outcome change attributable to a mark-scheme literacy intervention I designed.
I run network-wide mark-scheme deep-dive sessions at least twice per year.

activity.task: Conduct a 3-year mark-scheme alignment audit for your subject across the network. Identify one AO consistently underweighted by partner teachers. Design a 3-month intervention (CPD + SoW revision + exemplar library) with a target outcome metric. Present to your HQ Director.
activity.output: Audit report + intervention design + Director presentation slides.
activity.evidence: Upload the artefacts to your IPDL.

csm-1_lead.reading: At the Lead level, HQ Subject Specialists shape mark-scheme practice beyond the Eduversal network — engaging with Cambridge International directly (examiner training, regional working groups), publishing on Indonesian-context Cambridge teaching, and coaching other specialists on mark-scheme analysis methodology. A specialist at this stage holds at least one Cambridge-facing role (examiner, moderator, or endorser), contributes to Cambridge regional consultations on mark-scheme conventions, and develops the next generation of Eduversal Subject Specialists through structured mentoring. For example, a Chemistry specialist with 8 years' Eduversal experience holds a Cambridge IGCSE Chemistry (0620) examiner role for two years, brings detailed examiner-perspective insights back to her network, mentors three early-career Eduversal specialists through structured one-year coaching cycles, and presents at the regional Cambridge Schools Conference in Singapore on Indonesian-context Cambridge Chemistry teaching. This practice aligns with CTS 3.7 + 4.5 + 5.4 (external network engagement). At this stage, the specialist is shaping practice across institutional boundaries — Cambridge-side, Eduversal-side, and partner-school-side simultaneously.
keyTakeaways: Hold at least one Cambridge-facing role (examiner, moderator, endorser) — the perspective transforms your network coaching credibility.
Mentor early-career specialists through structured one-year cycles — your network's longevity depends on succession.
Publish or present from the Indonesian Cambridge context — local insight has international value Cambridge increasingly seeks.
selfAssessment: I hold or have held a Cambridge examiner / moderator / endorser role.
I mentor at least one early-career Eduversal specialist through a structured cycle.
I have presented at a regional Cambridge event or published on Cambridge teaching practice.
I bring Cambridge-side insights back to the network in structured ways (briefings, written notes, CPD).
I am known within my Cambridge subject community as an Indonesian-context expert.

activity.task: Apply for a Cambridge-facing role in your subject (examiner / moderator / endorser) if you do not already hold one. If you do, mentor an early-career Eduversal specialist through a 6-month coaching cycle. Document the mentoring/role engagement and bring three concrete insights back to a network-wide briefing.
activity.output: Role application/mentoring cycle log + network briefing notes.
activity.evidence: Upload the artefacts to your IPDL.

csm-2_awareness.reading: At the Awareness level, HQ Subject Specialists are beginning to understand Cambridge command words — the 13-15 directives ('describe', 'explain', 'evaluate', 'compare', 'justify', etc.) that determine what cognitive operation a question requires — as a teachable skill set that bridges question paper and student response. A specialist at this stage can name the command words for their subject, recognise that students often lose marks not because they lack subject knowledge but because they execute the wrong cognitive operation, and identify where partner teachers' lessons embed (or fail to embed) command-word fluency. For example, a Mathematics specialist in the Eduversal HQ team reviews a partner school's Year 10 IGCSE Mathematics (0580) mock papers from Bandung and notices that 30% of lost marks came from 'show that' questions where students gave the answer but did not show the working — a command-word literacy gap, not a content knowledge gap. This practice aligns with CTS 3.2 + 3.5 (subject pedagogy) and the Eduversal Specialist role in school appraisal d2 (Teaching Quality) evidence. At this stage, the specialist is building command-word literacy in her own observations but is not yet designing network-wide command-word teaching interventions.
keyTakeaways: Command words are a teachable cognitive-operation skill — not vocabulary tests.
Most lost marks in Cambridge papers are command-word execution failures, not content gaps.
Begin tracking command-word execution failures in your post-observation feedback.
selfAssessment: I can name all 13-15 Cambridge command words used in my subject.
I can explain the cognitive operation each command word requires.
I notice command-word execution failures in student work I review.
I have surfaced at least one command-word gap in a school-appraisal visit this term.
I can distinguish content gaps from command-word execution gaps in student error patterns.

activity.task: Review 20 student responses from a recent partner-school assessment in your subject. Classify each lost mark as content-gap, command-word-gap, or other. Compute the proportions. Identify the three most frequent command-word execution failures.
activity.output: Classified error analysis + three top command-word patterns.

csm-2_practitioner.reading: At the Practitioner level, HQ Subject Specialists consistently embed command-word literacy in partner-school visits and CPD sessions. A specialist at this stage runs structured command-word workshops, designs classroom routines partner teachers can adopt (command-word starter activities, command-word self-assessment in student work), and tracks command-word execution improvement in subsequent assessments. For example, a Biology specialist designs a 90-minute partner-school CPD on command-word literacy: she trains teachers in five Cambridge IGCSE Biology (0610) command words ('describe', 'explain', 'compare', 'suggest', 'evaluate'), provides student-facing handouts with worked examples, and asks each teacher to commit to embedding one command-word routine in the following week. She returns three weeks later and observes which routines stuck. This practice aligns with CTS 3.5 + 4.4. At this stage, the specialist runs effective command-word interventions within partner schools but does not yet shape network-level command-word standards or contribute to Cambridge community discussions on the topic.
keyTakeaways: Design command-word routines partner teachers can use in starters, plenaries, and student self-assessment.
Provide student-facing handouts — command words are a student vocabulary as much as a teacher vocabulary.
Return for follow-up observation 3-4 weeks after the workshop — that is when you find out if the routine stuck.
selfAssessment: I run structured command-word workshops at partner schools at least once per term.
I provide student-facing command-word resources to partner teachers.
I follow up workshops with observation 3-4 weeks later to verify uptake.
I track command-word execution in student work pre- and post-intervention.
I can describe one partner-school where command-word teaching has visibly improved.

activity.task: Design a 90-minute command-word workshop for partner-school teachers in your subject. Deliver it. Conduct follow-up observation 3-4 weeks later in at least two of the participating teachers' classrooms. Document workshop materials, follow-up observations, and what stuck.
activity.output: Workshop materials + follow-up observation log + uptake reflection.

csm-2_advanced.reading: At the Advanced level, HQ Subject Specialists systematically integrate command-word literacy into Eduversal network standards — designing the network-wide curriculum framework, partner-teacher induction expectations, and cross-school benchmarking that holds command-word teaching as a non-negotiable. A specialist at this stage works with HQ Directors to embed command-word literacy in Eduversal scheme-of-work templates, designs the new-teacher induction module on command-word teaching, and monitors network-wide command-word execution data via cross-school assessment analysis. For example, a Mathematics specialist with 5 years' Eduversal experience leads the redesign of the network's Year 9 Mathematics scheme of work to include explicit command-word starter routines in every unit, contributes to the new-teacher induction handbook with a command-word teaching module, and tracks year-on-year improvement in 'show that' question performance across the 14 IGCSE Mathematics partner schools. Network performance on 'show that' questions improves by 12 percentile points across two years. This practice aligns with CTS 3.5 + 4.5 + Eduversal Network Standards. At this stage, the specialist shapes network practice with measurable outcomes but does not yet contribute Cambridge-facing publications on command-word pedagogy.
keyTakeaways: Embed command-word teaching in Eduversal scheme-of-work templates — make it a default, not an optional add-on.
Track network-wide command-word execution via cross-school assessment analysis — outcomes prove the intervention worked.
Contribute to network induction modules — your network's command-word literacy depends on what new teachers learn from day one.
selfAssessment: Network scheme-of-work templates in my subject embed command-word routines.
I track network-wide command-word execution year-on-year.
I have contributed to new-teacher induction modules in my subject this year.
I can describe one specific outcome change attributable to network-wide command-word teaching standards.
I have presented network command-word findings to HQ Directors.

activity.task: Design a network-wide command-word standard for your subject's scheme-of-work template. Submit to HQ Director for approval. Track network-wide command-word execution data across two assessment cycles. Compare year-on-year improvement against pre-standard baseline.
activity.output: Network standard document + cross-school execution analysis + Director approval/feedback record.

csm-2_lead.reading: At the Lead level, HQ Subject Specialists shape command-word pedagogy beyond Eduversal — contributing to Cambridge community discussions on command-word teaching, presenting at regional conferences, and mentoring other specialists in network-level command-word work. A specialist at this stage holds Cambridge-facing roles (examiner, moderator) that include command-word interpretation responsibility, contributes to Cambridge regional working groups on command-word pedagogy, and writes for Cambridge community channels on Indonesian-context command-word teaching. For example, an English Language specialist with 9 years' Eduversal experience holds a Cambridge IGCSE First Language English (0500) examiner role, presents at the Cambridge Schools Conference Asia on 'command-word teaching in second-language Cambridge contexts', co-authors with a Cambridge Senior Examiner a paper on 'discuss' versus 'evaluate' interpretation in second-language candidate responses, and mentors three early-career specialists through structured command-word pedagogy cycles. This practice aligns with CTS 4.5 + 5.4. At this stage, the specialist is shaping command-word pedagogy at the international level — Cambridge-side, network-side, and emerging-specialist-side simultaneously.
keyTakeaways: Hold Cambridge-facing roles that include command-word interpretation responsibility.
Present and publish on Indonesian-context command-word pedagogy — the international Cambridge community values local insight.
Mentor early-career specialists through structured command-word pedagogy cycles.
selfAssessment: I hold a Cambridge-facing role with command-word interpretation responsibility.
I have presented at a regional Cambridge event on command-word pedagogy.
I have published or co-authored on Indonesian-context Cambridge command-word teaching.
I mentor at least one early-career specialist on command-word pedagogy work.
I am cited within Cambridge community discussions on Indonesian-context command-word teaching.

activity.task: Submit a presentation proposal to a regional Cambridge event on command-word pedagogy. If accepted, develop and deliver. If not, write a 1500-word piece for Cambridge community channels on Indonesian-context command-word teaching insights from your network.
activity.output: Presentation materials OR published article OR submission record + reflection on community response.

csm-3_awareness.reading: At the Awareness level, HQ Subject Specialists are beginning to read Cambridge Principal Examiner Reports as primary professional resources — not optional supplements. A specialist at this stage can locate the Principal Examiner Report for any recent paper in their subject, identify the recurring student errors the Chief Examiner names, and recognise that these reports name the same errors year after year because the underlying teaching gaps persist. For example, a Physics specialist in the Eduversal HQ team reads the Cambridge IGCSE Physics (0625) May/June Principal Examiner Report and notices the Chief Examiner has named 'students confuse mass and weight' as a top-three error for four consecutive series — yet partner-school Physics teachers in the network still teach the distinction in a single 30-minute lesson at the start of Year 9 and never revisit it. The report names the gap; the network has not closed it. This practice aligns with CTS 3.7 + 4.5. At this stage, the specialist is reading the reports but is not yet systematically mining them for partner-school intervention design.
keyTakeaways: Principal Examiner Reports name recurring student errors that persist across years — read them as professional resources, not as one-off summaries.
Recurring errors signal persistent teaching gaps that single lessons rarely close.
Begin a personal log of recurring errors named in the reports for your subject.
selfAssessment: I have read the most recent Principal Examiner Report for every paper in my subject.
I can name three recurring errors the Chief Examiner has cited across multiple series.
I notice when partner-school teaching does not address recurring report-named errors.
I keep a personal log of recurring errors named in the reports for my subject.
I can explain to a partner teacher why a specific error is structural, not individual.

activity.task: Read the last three Principal Examiner Reports for one paper in your subject. List every recurring error named in two or more of them. Cross-reference your list against partner-school scheme-of-work coverage of these topics. Identify three errors that are systematically under-taught in the network.
activity.output: Recurring error log + scheme-of-work cross-reference + three under-taught topics.

csm-3_practitioner.reading: At the Practitioner level, HQ Subject Specialists consistently mine Principal Examiner Reports for partner-school intervention design. A specialist at this stage uses report findings to design specific CPD sessions, scheme-of-work amendments, and partner-school visit focus areas — turning report-named errors into structural teaching responses. For example, a Chemistry specialist reads the IGCSE Chemistry (0620) Principal Examiner Report and notices a recurring error: students confuse the terms 'molecule' and 'compound'. She designs a 60-minute partner-school CPD module on conceptual-vocabulary precision in Chemistry teaching, develops a student-facing diagnostic activity that surfaces the misconception, and works with three partner schools to embed the routine in their Year 9 scheme of work. Six months later, partner-school formative assessment data shows the misconception rate drop from 40% to 12% in those schools. This practice aligns with CTS 3.7 + 4.4. At this stage, the specialist runs report-driven interventions effectively at school level but does not yet drive network-wide systematic responses to multiple recurring errors.
keyTakeaways: Translate report-named errors into specific, named CPD interventions — vague 'we should address misconceptions' is not enough.
Design student-facing diagnostic activities that surface the misconception explicitly.
Track misconception rate pre- and post-intervention in partner-school formative data.
selfAssessment: I design at least one report-driven CPD intervention per term.
I provide student-facing diagnostic activities for the misconceptions I target.
I track misconception rate change in partner-school formative data.
I can name three specific interventions I designed based on Principal Examiner Reports this year.
I follow up partner-school interventions 3-6 months later to verify durability.

activity.task: Choose one recurring error named in the Principal Examiner Report for your subject. Design a 60-90 minute partner-school CPD module addressing it. Include a student-facing diagnostic, scheme-of-work amendment, and follow-up data check 3 months later. Run the cycle in at least two partner schools.
activity.output: CPD materials + scheme amendment + follow-up data check across two schools.

csm-3_advanced.reading: At the Advanced level, HQ Subject Specialists systematically transform Principal Examiner Reports into network-wide curriculum responses. A specialist at this stage maintains a multi-year report analysis that identifies persistent network-wide teaching gaps, designs network-wide intervention programmes (CPD pathway + scheme-of-work standards + assessment moderation focus), and tracks year-on-year improvement against report-named errors at the network level. For example, a Mathematics specialist with 6 years' Eduversal experience analyses 4 years of IGCSE Mathematics (0580) Principal Examiner Reports across all 14 partner schools, identifies five persistent network-wide teaching gaps (e.g., probability tree diagrams, geometric proof, algebraic manipulation under examination conditions), and leads a 2-year network-wide intervention covering all 14 schools. After 2 years, network-wide candidate performance on the five identified gap topics improves by 14 percentile points. This practice aligns with CTS 3.7 + 4.5 + Eduversal Network Standards. At this stage, the specialist shapes network curriculum responses with measurable multi-year outcomes but does not yet contribute Cambridge-facing publications on Indonesian-context examiner-report responsiveness.
keyTakeaways: Maintain a multi-year report analysis — single-report responses are tactical; multi-year analysis is strategic.
Design network-wide intervention programmes that span scheme of work + CPD + moderation simultaneously.
Track year-on-year network-wide outcome change against report-named errors — this is the proof your strategy works.
selfAssessment: I maintain a multi-year analysis of Principal Examiner Reports for my subject.
I have designed at least one network-wide intervention programme this year.
I track year-on-year network-wide outcome change against report-named errors.
Network-wide candidate performance on report-named errors has improved during my tenure.
I have presented network-wide examiner-report findings to HQ Directors.

activity.task: Conduct a 4-year Principal Examiner Report analysis for your subject. Identify three persistent network-wide teaching gaps. Design a 2-year network-wide intervention programme addressing them. Submit the design to HQ Director for resource approval.
activity.output: Multi-year analysis + 2-year intervention programme design + Director approval record.

csm-3_lead.reading: At the Lead level, HQ Subject Specialists shape examiner-report responsiveness as a discipline beyond Eduversal — contributing to Cambridge community discussions on examiner-report use, mentoring other specialists in multi-year analysis methodology, and publishing on Indonesian-context examiner-report responsiveness. A specialist at this stage holds Cambridge-facing roles that include report-writing perspective, contributes to Cambridge regional working groups on examiner-report communication, and writes for Cambridge community channels on multi-year report analysis methodology. For example, a Biology specialist with 8 years' Eduversal experience holds a Cambridge IGCSE Biology (0610) examiner role for 3 years, contributes a chapter to a Cambridge community publication on 'using Principal Examiner Reports as systemic teaching guides', presents at a regional Cambridge conference on 'multi-year examiner-report analysis from a national network perspective', and mentors two early-career specialists through structured 4-year report analysis cycles. This practice aligns with CTS 4.5 + 5.4. At this stage, the specialist is shaping examiner-report responsiveness across institutional boundaries — Cambridge-side, network-side, and emerging-specialist-side simultaneously.
keyTakeaways: Hold Cambridge-facing roles that include report-writing perspective.
Publish or present on multi-year examiner-report analysis methodology.
Mentor early-career specialists through structured multi-year analysis cycles.
selfAssessment: I hold a Cambridge-facing role that includes report-writing perspective.
I have published or contributed to publications on examiner-report responsiveness.
I have presented at regional Cambridge events on multi-year analysis.
I mentor at least one early-career specialist through structured analysis cycles.
I am known within Cambridge community discussions on Indonesian-context examiner-report use.

activity.task: Submit a publication or presentation proposal to a Cambridge community channel or regional event on multi-year examiner-report analysis methodology. Mentor one early-career specialist through a structured 4-year analysis cycle.
activity.output: Submission/publication record + mentoring cycle log.

csm-4_awareness.reading: At the Awareness level, HQ Subject Specialists are beginning to maintain currency with subject-discipline research — reading peer-reviewed journals, professional association communications, and current research that shapes how their subject is taught at international level. A specialist at this stage can name two or three current research questions in their subject discipline, recognises that subject knowledge is not static (new findings, new pedagogical insights, new applications emerge regularly), and reads at least one professional source per month. For example, a Mathematics specialist in the Eduversal HQ team subscribes to the journal of the National Council of Teachers of Mathematics, reads its monthly digests, and discovers recent research on the role of visualisation in algebraic understanding. She begins thinking about how visualisation routines could be embedded in Eduversal's IGCSE Mathematics scheme of work. This practice aligns with CTS 4.5 (research-engaged practice). At this stage, the specialist is reading research but not yet translating it into network-level scheme-of-work decisions or partner-school CPD.
keyTakeaways: Subject knowledge is not static — current research changes how the subject is taught at international level.
Subscribe to at least one peer-reviewed source or professional association in your subject.
Begin a personal log: research insights → potential network application.
selfAssessment: I subscribe to at least one peer-reviewed source or professional association in my subject.
I read at least one professional source per month.
I can name three current research questions in my subject discipline.
I keep a personal log of research insights with potential network application.
I have shared at least one research insight with my Director or peers this term.

activity.task: Subscribe to one peer-reviewed journal or professional association in your subject. Read three articles from the most recent issues. Identify one finding with potential network application. Discuss with your Director.
activity.output: Subscription record + three article notes + one application proposal.

csm-4_practitioner.reading: At the Practitioner level, HQ Subject Specialists consistently translate subject-discipline research into network-level practice. A specialist at this stage maintains a structured reading practice (~2 hours per week), brings research insights to partner-school CPD design, and influences scheme-of-work amendments based on current findings. For example, a Biology specialist reads recent research on argumentation pedagogy in science (Osborne, Erduran), recognises that partner-school IGCSE Biology (0610) lessons rarely include explicit argumentation routines, and designs a partner-school CPD module on argumentation in scientific reasoning. She implements the module across three partner schools and integrates the routine into the Year 10 scheme-of-work template. This practice aligns with CTS 4.5 + 4.4. At this stage, the specialist translates research effectively into specific interventions but does not yet contribute network-wide research-engagement standards or co-author publications.
keyTakeaways: Maintain a structured reading practice — ~2 hours per week is realistic and sustainable.
Translate research into specific interventions — vague 'evidence-based' is not enough.
Influence scheme-of-work amendments based on current findings — research stays academic until it shapes daily teaching.
selfAssessment: I maintain a structured reading practice of ~2 hours per week.
I bring research insights to at least two partner-school CPD designs per year.
I have influenced at least one scheme-of-work amendment based on current research.
I can name three research-derived interventions I designed this year.
I share research insights with my Director and peers regularly.

activity.task: Choose one current research insight from your subject discipline. Design a partner-school CPD module that translates it into classroom routines. Implement in at least two partner schools. Document outcome.
activity.output: CPD module + implementation log + outcome reflection.

csm-4_advanced.reading: At the Advanced level, HQ Subject Specialists systematically integrate research-engagement into Eduversal network strategy. A specialist at this stage maintains a network-level research-engagement plan, contributes to Eduversal subject pacing standards with research justification, and ensures that scheme-of-work decisions are research-grounded rather than tradition-grounded. For example, an English Language specialist designs a network-wide research-engagement programme: every two months, she selects one peer-reviewed paper relevant to IGCSE First Language English (0500) teaching, summarises it for partner-school subject leaders, and runs a 30-minute discussion at the next subject leaders' network meeting. After two years, network scheme-of-work amendments are consistently research-justified, and partner-school subject leaders cite specific research findings in their own teaching decisions. This practice aligns with CTS 4.5 + Eduversal Network Standards. At this stage, the specialist shapes network research-engagement culture but does not yet contribute Cambridge-facing publications or position the network as a research-contributor (rather than research-consumer).
keyTakeaways: Maintain a network-level research-engagement plan — episodic reading is not enough.
Run network research-discussion sessions — collective sense-making accelerates uptake.
Ground scheme-of-work decisions in research — tradition-grounded curriculum is the default; research-grounded is a specialist's contribution.
selfAssessment: I maintain a network-level research-engagement plan.
I run network research-discussion sessions at least every two months.
Network scheme-of-work decisions in my subject are research-justified.
Partner-school subject leaders cite specific research in their teaching decisions.
I have presented network research-engagement findings to HQ Directors.

activity.task: Design a 6-month network research-engagement plan for your subject. Identify three research papers, three subject-leader discussion sessions, and one scheme-of-work amendment that will result. Implement and track uptake.
activity.output: Plan + implementation log + uptake reflection.

csm-4_lead.reading: At the Lead level, HQ Subject Specialists position the Eduversal network as a research-contributor — not just a research-consumer. A specialist at this stage publishes on Indonesian-context Cambridge teaching from the network's accumulated practice insight, presents at international conferences, and contributes to academic discussions on subject pedagogy. For example, a Chemistry specialist with 9 years' Eduversal experience publishes a peer-reviewed article on 'language demand in Cambridge IGCSE Chemistry assessment for second-language candidates', presents at the regional International Conference on Chemical Education on Indonesian-context Cambridge Chemistry teaching, and contributes a chapter to a forthcoming book on Cambridge teaching practice in South-East Asia. This practice aligns with CTS 4.5 + 5.4. At this stage, the specialist is positioning the network as an international contributor to subject pedagogy.
keyTakeaways: Publish on Indonesian-context Cambridge teaching — your network's accumulated insight has international value.
Present at international conferences — visibility positions the network as a research contributor.
Contribute to academic discussions on subject pedagogy — Indonesia perspective enriches international discourse.
selfAssessment: I have published or co-authored a peer-reviewed article on subject pedagogy.
I have presented at an international conference in my subject discipline.
I am cited in academic discussions on Cambridge teaching in my subject.
I contribute regularly to subject-discipline academic channels.
I am known within my subject's international community as an Indonesia-context expert.

activity.task: Submit a publication proposal to a peer-reviewed journal or international conference in your subject discipline. If accepted, develop and deliver. Document the international engagement and reflect on what changed in your network as a result.
activity.output: Publication/presentation record + reflection on network impact.

cof-1_awareness.reading: At the Awareness level, HQ Subject Specialists are beginning to understand structured lesson observation as a discipline of evidence collection — not impression formation. A specialist at this stage can use a structured observation rubric (Eduversal Walkthrough Rubric or Danielson Framework for Teaching), recognises the difference between low-inference scripted observation ('At 10:14 the teacher asked X') and high-inference impression ('the teacher seemed nervous'), and is beginning to script lessons with timestamps and verbatim quotes. For example, a newly appointed Physics specialist visits a Year 10 IGCSE Physics (0625) lesson in Bandung; her instinct is to write 'the lesson was good but the questioning could be deeper'. She catches herself, opens her structured rubric, and instead scripts: '10:14 — T asks: What is acceleration?' / 'Three students answer in chorus: speed change.' / '10:14:30 — T accepts answer; no follow-up question.' This is evidence she can act on. This practice aligns with CTS 4.1 + 4.4 + Eduversal Domain 4 (Monitoring) standards. At this stage, the specialist is building scripting habits but not yet using the evidence consistently in feedback design.
keyTakeaways: Lesson observation is evidence collection, not impression formation — script verbatim with timestamps.
Low-inference observation is the foundation of fair, useful feedback.
Practice the discipline: 'I noticed at 10:14 that...' — never 'It seemed that...'
selfAssessment: I use a structured observation rubric on every classroom visit.
I script verbatim with timestamps, not impressions.
I can distinguish low-inference observations from high-inference judgements in my own notes.
I have re-read my observation notes from a year ago and noticed where I confused inference for evidence.
I know the Eduversal Walkthrough Rubric and Danielson FfT well enough to use either fluently.

activity.task: Conduct three classroom observations using the Eduversal Walkthrough Rubric. Script verbatim with timestamps. Re-read each observation log 24 hours later and circle every high-inference statement. Rewrite each as low-inference scripted observation.
activity.output: Three observation logs + rewrite analysis.

cof-1_practitioner.reading: At the Practitioner level, HQ Subject Specialists consistently produce low-inference scripted observations that anchor effective post-observation feedback. A specialist at this stage observes lessons fluently against a structured rubric, captures verbatim evidence at scale (15-25 timestamps in a 40-minute lesson), and builds a portfolio of scripted observations that document partner-school teaching across the network. For example, a Biology specialist visiting a partner school in Surabaya conducts three lessons in one day, producing three detailed scripted observation logs with 60+ timestamps total, and is able to draw cross-lesson patterns ('Domain 3 questioning is strong across all three teachers; Domain 5 feedback is uneven') from accumulated evidence. This practice aligns with CTS 4.1 + 4.4. At this stage, the specialist observes effectively at scale but does not yet train other observers (peers, AH leaders) in the discipline.
keyTakeaways: Low-inference scripted observation scales to multiple lessons per visit — the discipline becomes faster with practice.
Cross-lesson pattern recognition emerges from accumulated scripted evidence — single-lesson observations are diagnostic; multi-lesson observations are systemic.
Build a portfolio of scripted observations — over time it becomes a network teaching evidence base.
selfAssessment: I observe and script 2-3 lessons per visit on average.
I produce 15-25 verbatim timestamps per 40-minute lesson.
I draw cross-lesson patterns from accumulated observation evidence.
I maintain a personal portfolio of scripted observations.
Other specialists or AH leaders refer to my observation discipline as a benchmark.

activity.task: Conduct a school-appraisal visit observing three lessons in one day. Produce detailed scripted observations for all three. Draw three cross-lesson patterns from the accumulated evidence. Use the patterns in your school-appraisal feedback.
activity.output: Three observation logs + cross-lesson pattern analysis + feedback artefact.

cof-1_advanced.reading: At the Advanced level, HQ Subject Specialists systematically train other observers in the scripted observation discipline. A specialist at this stage runs observer training sessions for AH leaders (school principals, academic coordinators, subject leaders), develops calibration exercises that reveal inter-rater reliability gaps, and contributes to the Eduversal network's overall observation capacity. For example, an English Language specialist designs a half-day observer training programme for partner-school subject leaders: she shows examples of low-inference vs high-inference observations, runs a video-based calibration exercise where participants observe the same lesson clip and compare scripted notes, and provides a follow-up feedback cycle where she reviews each subject leader's first three live observations and provides specific 'discipline coaching'. This practice aligns with CTS 4.4 + 4.5 + Eduversal Network Standards on observer calibration. At this stage, the specialist trains observers within the network effectively but does not yet contribute Cambridge-facing or international observer-training methodology.
keyTakeaways: Train other observers in the scripted discipline — your network's observation capacity scales through training, not single-handed effort.
Use video-based calibration exercises — same lesson, multiple observers, comparison of scripted notes reveals reliability gaps.
Provide follow-up feedback cycles to early-career observers — discipline rarely consolidates without coaching.
selfAssessment: I run observer training programmes for AH leaders at least once per year.
I use video-based calibration exercises in observer training.
I provide follow-up coaching to early-career observers in the network.
I track inter-rater reliability across the observer cohort I train.
I have measurably improved network observation discipline through my training work.

activity.task: Design and deliver a half-day observer training programme for AH leaders in your subject. Include video-based calibration. Provide follow-up coaching to at least three trained observers across the next three months. Track their observation discipline improvement.
activity.output: Training programme materials + calibration exercise + follow-up coaching log.

cof-1_lead.reading: At the Lead level, HQ Subject Specialists shape observer-training methodology beyond the Eduversal network — contributing to Cambridge community discussions on observer calibration, presenting at international Cambridge conferences on multi-observer training, and mentoring early-career specialists in observer training methodology. For example, a Mathematics specialist with 9 years' Eduversal experience holds a Cambridge moderator role that includes observer training (training other moderators in scoring discipline) for 2 years, presents at the Cambridge Schools Conference Asia on 'multi-observer calibration training in cross-cultural Cambridge contexts', and writes for Cambridge community channels on observer-training methodology insights from the Indonesian Cambridge context. The Indonesian-context observer-training methodology — particularly the structured calibration sequence using video-based exercises before live paired observations — influences how Cambridge thinks about observer training across regional networks.
keyTakeaways: Hold Cambridge-facing roles that include observer training.
Publish or present on multi-observer calibration in cross-cultural Cambridge contexts.
Mentor early-career specialists in observer training methodology.
selfAssessment: I hold a Cambridge-facing role that includes observer training.
I have presented at a regional Cambridge event on observer methodology.
I mentor at least one early-career specialist in observer training.
I contribute to Cambridge regional working groups on observation.
I am known within Cambridge community discussions on cross-cultural observation methodology.

activity.task: Submit a presentation proposal to a regional Cambridge event on multi-observer calibration in cross-cultural Cambridge contexts. Mentor one early-career specialist in observer training methodology.
activity.output: Submission record + mentoring cycle log.

cof-2_awareness.reading: At the Awareness level, HQ Subject Specialists are beginning to understand structured post-observation feedback — specifically the Glow / Grow / Go protocol — as a developmental practice that lands differently from intuitive feedback. A specialist at this stage can name the three components (Glow = strength named with evidence; Grow = growth area named concretely; Go = single, specific next-step the teacher can act on this week), recognises that intuitive 'overall, the lesson was good but...' feedback rarely changes practice, and is beginning to use the Glow / Grow / Go format in her own post-observation conversations. For example, a Mathematics specialist visits a Year 9 IGCSE Mathematics (0580) lesson in Yogyakarta; instead of saying 'the lesson was clear but the differentiation was weak', she frames: 'Glow: at 10:18 you provided three worked examples at increasing difficulty — your sequencing was strong. Grow: students at the lower attainment band copied the worked examples without scaffolding. Go: try providing a partially-worked second example with a specific gap for those students to fill in next Tuesday.' This practice aligns with CTS 4.4 + Eduversal Walkthrough Rubric standards. At this stage, the specialist uses the format consistently but is not yet measurably tracking whether 'Go' actions land in practice.
keyTakeaways: Glow / Grow / Go is a structured developmental feedback protocol — use it consistently, not as one option among many.
The 'Go' component must be specific, single, and actionable this week — broad goals don't stick.
Begin tracking whether 'Go' actions actually appear in your follow-up observation.
selfAssessment: I use the Glow / Grow / Go format in every post-observation conversation.
I can name the difference between concrete and abstract 'Go' actions.
I cite specific timestamped evidence in my Glow component.
I name 'Grow' areas concretely, not vaguely.
I have followed up to see whether at least one 'Go' action actually landed.

activity.task: Conduct three post-observation conversations using the Glow / Grow / Go format. Document each Go action. Return for follow-up observation 2-3 weeks later. Verify whether each Go action actually landed in practice.
activity.output: Three Glow / Grow / Go feedback logs + follow-up observation results.

cof-2_practitioner.reading: At the Practitioner level, HQ Subject Specialists consistently produce Glow / Grow / Go feedback that lands in changed practice. A specialist at this stage tracks Go-action landing rate (proportion of Go actions actually visible in follow-up observation), adjusts coaching based on what is and is not landing, and uses Go-landing data to improve her own feedback design. For example, a Physics specialist tracks Go actions across one semester of partner-school visits and notices that 'Go' actions framed as 'try this single specific routine on a specific day' land at 70%, while 'Go' actions framed as 'develop your questioning further' land at 15%. The data refines her feedback design. This practice aligns with CTS 4.4 + Eduversal Domain 6 (collaboration / coaching) standards. At this stage, the specialist refines feedback effectively but does not yet train other observers in feedback design or shape network feedback culture.
keyTakeaways: Track Go-action landing rate — outcomes prove the feedback design.
Specific, single, named-day Go actions land far more reliably than abstract goals.
Use Go-landing data to refine your own feedback design — this is professional learning, not just teacher coaching.
selfAssessment: I track Go-action landing rate across observations.
I refine my feedback design based on landing-rate data.
Go-action landing rate in my coaching is above 50%.
I can describe what kinds of Go actions land and what kinds do not in my coaching.
I share my Go-landing data with my Director or peers for reflection.

activity.task: Track Go-action landing across one semester of school-appraisal visits. At semester end, analyse the landing rates by feedback type. Identify three patterns. Refine your feedback design and pilot the refinements over one month.
activity.output: Landing rate dataset + pattern analysis + refined feedback design.

cof-2_advanced.reading: At the Advanced level, HQ Subject Specialists systematically train other observers in feedback design — particularly the Glow / Grow / Go protocol and Go-action specificity. A specialist at this stage runs network-wide feedback training, develops feedback exemplars that demonstrate effective vs ineffective Go actions, and shapes the network's overall feedback culture. For example, a Biology specialist designs a network-wide CPD on Glow / Grow / Go feedback, develops a library of 30 example Go actions (15 effective + 15 to-avoid) drawn from real partner-school observations, runs the CPD with 22 AH leaders across 14 partner schools, and tracks Go-action landing rate improvement across those leaders for one year. After one year, network-average Go-landing rate improves from 40% to 65%. This practice aligns with CTS 4.4 + 4.5 + Eduversal Network Standards. At this stage, the specialist shapes network feedback culture with measurable outcomes but does not yet contribute Cambridge-facing publications on feedback methodology.
keyTakeaways: Train AH leaders in feedback design — your network's feedback capacity scales through training.
Develop an exemplar library — abstract advice rarely changes practice; concrete examples do.
Track network-wide Go-landing rate improvement — outcomes prove the training worked.
selfAssessment: I run network-wide feedback design training at least once per year.
I maintain a library of feedback exemplars.
I track network-wide Go-action landing rate.
Network-average Go-landing rate has improved during my tenure.
AH leaders cite specific feedback design improvements from my training.

activity.task: Design and deliver a network-wide CPD on Glow / Grow / Go feedback design. Include an exemplar library. Track landing-rate improvement across the trained cohort for 6 months. Compare against pre-training baseline.
activity.output: CPD design + exemplar library + landing-rate analysis.

cof-2_lead.reading: At the Lead level, HQ Subject Specialists shape feedback methodology beyond the Eduversal network — contributing to Cambridge community discussions on observation feedback, presenting at international Cambridge conferences on cross-cultural feedback design, and mentoring specialists from other Cambridge regional networks in feedback methodology. For example, an English Language specialist with 9 years' Eduversal experience presents at the Cambridge Schools Conference Asia on 'Glow / Grow / Go feedback design across cross-cultural Cambridge contexts: methodology and outcome data from a 14-school Indonesian network', writes for Cambridge community channels on cross-cultural feedback design insights, and mentors two early-career specialists from regional Cambridge networks (Vietnam, Thailand) through structured feedback methodology cycles. The Indonesian-context feedback methodology insights influence how Cambridge thinks about feedback across regional networks. This practice aligns with Cambridge International Teacher Standard 4.5 + 5.4.
keyTakeaways: Hold Cambridge-facing roles that include feedback responsibility.
Publish or present on feedback design in cross-cultural Cambridge contexts.
Mentor early-career specialists in feedback methodology.
selfAssessment: I hold a Cambridge-facing role that includes feedback responsibility.
I have presented at a regional Cambridge event on observation feedback.
I mentor at least one early-career specialist in feedback methodology.
I contribute to Cambridge regional discussions on feedback design.
I am known within Cambridge community for cross-cultural feedback methodology insight.

activity.task: Submit a presentation proposal to a regional Cambridge event on cross-cultural feedback design. Mentor one early-career specialist in feedback methodology.
activity.output: Submission record + mentoring cycle log.

cof-3_awareness.reading: At the Awareness level, HQ Subject Specialists are beginning to understand inter-rater reliability — the consistency with which different observers score the same lesson — as a discipline that requires structured calibration, not assumed agreement. A specialist at this stage can recognise that two observers watching the same lesson can score it 1.5 levels apart on the same rubric, that this divergence undermines the credibility of the entire observation system, and that calibration is the structural fix. For example, a Mathematics specialist conducts a paired observation with an AH academic coordinator at a partner school in Bandung; they watch a Year 11 lesson together and produce independent scoring against the Eduversal Walkthrough Rubric. The specialist scores Domain 3 (Instructional Delivery) as Level 3; the coordinator scores it as Level 2. They debrief and discover they were applying the rubric criteria differently. This practice aligns with CTS 4.4 + 4.5 + Eduversal Domain 4 (Monitoring) calibration standards. At this stage, the specialist recognises calibration as essential but does not yet run structured calibration cycles.
keyTakeaways: Inter-rater reliability is not assumed — two observers can score the same lesson 1.5 levels apart without calibration.
Paired observations followed by debrief are the foundational calibration practice.
Begin tracking your own scoring against another observer's on the same lesson.
selfAssessment: I have conducted at least one paired observation in the past term.
I can describe a specific scoring divergence I encountered and how we resolved it.
I recognise that calibration is structural, not optional.
I have debriefed at least one paired observation in detail.
I keep notes on calibration patterns I observe.

activity.task: Conduct a paired observation with another observer (peer specialist or AH leader). Score independently against the Eduversal Walkthrough Rubric. Debrief any divergence. Document the divergence pattern and the resolution reasoning.
activity.output: Paired observation log + divergence analysis + resolution notes.

cof-3_practitioner.reading: At the Practitioner level, HQ Subject Specialists consistently run structured calibration cycles — paired observations, video-based calibration, calibrated norming sessions — as part of their regular practice. A specialist at this stage tracks her own inter-rater reliability against peer specialists, identifies and addresses systematic scoring biases (e.g., 'I tend to score Domain 4 higher than my peers'), and uses calibration data to improve her observation discipline. For example, a Chemistry specialist runs a quarterly calibration session with two peer specialists: they all watch the same recorded lesson clip, score independently, then compare. Across four sessions in one year, her cross-rater reliability improves from 0.62 (κ) to 0.84. This practice aligns with CTS 4.4 + 4.5. At this stage, the specialist runs calibration effectively for herself but does not yet design calibration systems for the wider network observer cohort.
keyTakeaways: Run quarterly calibration sessions — episodic calibration is not enough; rhythm matters.
Track your own inter-rater reliability — outcomes prove the discipline works.
Identify and address systematic scoring biases — they don't go away on their own.
selfAssessment: I run quarterly calibration sessions with peer specialists.
I track my own inter-rater reliability across calibration sessions.
I can name specific systematic scoring biases I have addressed.
My inter-rater reliability has improved measurably during my tenure.
I share my calibration data with my Director for reflection.

activity.task: Run a quarterly calibration session with two peer specialists. Use a recorded lesson clip. Score independently. Calculate inter-rater reliability. Identify systematic biases. Plan one specific action to address each.
activity.output: Calibration session record + reliability calculation + bias analysis + action plan.

cof-3_advanced.reading: At the Advanced level, HQ Subject Specialists systematically design and run calibration systems for the wider Eduversal observer cohort. A specialist at this stage builds calibration infrastructure: video libraries, scoring keys, norming exercises, recurring calibration meetings, and systematic inter-rater reliability tracking across all observers in the network. For example, a Biology specialist designs a year-long calibration programme for 28 partner-school subject leaders + 7 HQ specialists: she builds a video library of 12 calibrated lessons (each with consensus scoring), runs quarterly virtual calibration sessions, and tracks inter-rater reliability across the cohort. After one year, network-average inter-rater reliability improves from 0.55 to 0.76. This practice aligns with CTS 4.4 + 4.5 + Eduversal Network Standards. At this stage, the specialist designs and runs network calibration but does not yet contribute Cambridge-facing publications on cross-cultural calibration methodology.
keyTakeaways: Build calibration infrastructure — video libraries, scoring keys, recurring sessions are the structure.
Track network-wide inter-rater reliability — outcomes prove the system.
Run network calibration as a year-long programme, not a one-off event.
selfAssessment: I have built or maintained a calibration video library for my subject network.
I run quarterly virtual calibration sessions with the network observer cohort.
I track network-wide inter-rater reliability.
Network-average inter-rater reliability has improved during my tenure.
I have presented network calibration findings to HQ Directors.

activity.task: Design a year-long calibration programme for your network observer cohort. Include video library + recurring sessions + reliability tracking. Implement one quarter. Document.
activity.output: Programme design + video library record + first quarter implementation log.

cof-3_lead.reading: At the Lead level, HQ Subject Specialists shape calibration methodology beyond the Eduversal network — contributing to Cambridge community discussions on cross-cultural observer calibration, presenting at international Cambridge conferences, and mentoring specialists from other Cambridge regional networks in calibration system design. For example, a Mathematics specialist with 9 years' Eduversal experience presents at a regional Cambridge event on 'multi-observer calibration in cross-cultural Cambridge contexts: methodology and 3-year inter-rater reliability data from a 16-school Indonesian network', writes for Cambridge community channels on cross-cultural calibration methodology insights, and mentors two early-career specialists from regional Cambridge networks (Singapore, Vietnam) through structured calibration system design cycles. The Indonesian-context calibration methodology — particularly cross-cultural inter-rater reliability tracking — influences how Cambridge thinks about observation methodology across regional networks. This practice aligns with Cambridge International Teacher Standard 4.5 + 5.4. At this stage, the specialist shapes calibration methodology at the international Cambridge community level.
keyTakeaways: Contribute to Cambridge community discussions on cross-cultural calibration.
Publish or present on inter-rater reliability methodology.
Mentor early-career specialists in calibration system design.
selfAssessment: I have presented at a regional Cambridge event on calibration methodology.
I contribute to Cambridge regional discussions on observer reliability.
I mentor at least one early-career specialist in calibration system design.
I am cited within Cambridge community discussions on cross-cultural calibration.
I have published or co-authored on calibration methodology.

activity.task: Submit a presentation proposal to a regional Cambridge event on cross-cultural calibration methodology. Mentor one early-career specialist through a calibration system design cycle.
activity.output: Submission record + mentoring cycle log.

cof-4_awareness.reading: At the Awareness level, HQ Subject Specialists are beginning to understand coaching impact tracking — measuring whether the post-observation Go actions they recommend actually change practice over time — as the missing layer between feedback and outcomes. A specialist at this stage can recognise that giving feedback once and never returning is a different practice from giving feedback and following up 3, 6, 12 weeks later. For example, an English Language specialist at a partner school in Solo gives a Go action ('add a structured 5-minute peer-review routine in the closing of writing lessons'), and 6 weeks later returns to the same teacher's classroom: she observes whether the routine landed, how it has evolved, and whether it has improved student writing quality. This practice aligns with CTS 4.1 + 4.5 + Eduversal Domain 4 (Monitoring) follow-up standards. At this stage, the specialist follows up but does not yet maintain a structured coaching impact log.
keyTakeaways: Coaching without follow-up is feedback theatre — the impact track is the substance.
Return for follow-up observation 3, 6, 12 weeks after a Go action — at each interval different things become visible.
Begin a structured coaching impact log: Go action → follow-up date → what landed.
selfAssessment: I follow up post-observation feedback 3-6 weeks later.
I keep a structured log of Go actions and what landed.
I can describe at least three specific Go actions that landed and three that did not.
I notice patterns in what kinds of Go actions stick.
I share follow-up findings with the partner teacher to surface mutual learning.

activity.task: Begin a structured coaching impact log for one term. For every post-observation Go action, schedule a 4-week follow-up. Document what you observe. At term end, review and identify three patterns.
activity.output: Coaching impact log + term-end pattern analysis.

cof-4_practitioner.reading: At the Practitioner level, HQ Subject Specialists consistently track coaching impact across multiple visits and adjust coaching plans based on what lands. A specialist at this stage maintains a teacher-by-teacher coaching impact log, recognises when a coaching plan is not working (Go actions consistently don't land for the same teacher), and adjusts coaching strategy accordingly. For example, a Mathematics specialist coaching a partner-school teacher in Tangerang tracks Go-action landing across 4 visits over 6 months: visits 1-2 show landing (formative routine adopted, command-word starter activity adopted), but visits 3-4 show stagnation (questioning depth Go action repeatedly not landing). The specialist recognises that the questioning Go action needs different scaffolding — perhaps modelling, perhaps co-planning — and adjusts the coaching approach. This practice aligns with CTS 4.1 + 4.4. At this stage, the specialist tracks impact effectively per teacher but does not yet design network-wide coaching pathway tracking.
keyTakeaways: Maintain a teacher-by-teacher coaching impact log — the patterns are individual, not network-level.
When the same Go action consistently fails to land, the coaching strategy needs adjustment, not the teacher.
Adjust coaching approach: more modelling? Co-planning? Different scaffolding?
selfAssessment: I maintain a teacher-by-teacher coaching impact log.
I recognise when a coaching plan is not working.
I adjust coaching approach when Go actions consistently fail to land.
I track at least three teachers' coaching impact across 6+ months.
I can describe one coaching plan I adjusted based on impact data.

activity.task: Maintain teacher-by-teacher coaching impact logs for at least three partner-school teachers across one semester. Identify one teacher whose coaching plan needs adjustment. Design and implement the adjustment. Track outcome.
activity.output: Three coaching impact logs + adjustment design + outcome reflection.

cof-4_advanced.reading: At the Advanced level, HQ Subject Specialists design and run network-wide coaching impact systems. A specialist at this stage builds coaching pathway infrastructure (teacher-by-teacher tracking + network aggregation + Director-facing reporting), uses data to identify network-wide coaching effectiveness patterns, and refines the network coaching framework based on outcomes. For example, a Physics specialist runs a network-wide coaching impact system across 14 partner schools and 22 IGCSE Physics (0625) teachers; she tracks Go-action landing rate per teacher per visit, identifies that 'questioning depth' Go actions consistently fail to land across the network, and redesigns the network's questioning CPD to address the gap. After one year, questioning Go-action landing rate improves from 25% to 55%. This practice aligns with CTS 4.4 + 4.5 + Eduversal Network Standards. At this stage, the specialist designs network coaching systems but does not yet contribute Cambridge-facing publications on coaching impact methodology.
keyTakeaways: Build network coaching pathway infrastructure — teacher tracking + aggregation + Director reporting.
Use data to identify network-wide coaching effectiveness patterns.
Refine the network coaching framework based on outcomes.
selfAssessment: I have built a network coaching impact tracking system.
I aggregate coaching impact data across the network.
I report network coaching effectiveness to HQ Directors.
Network-wide coaching impact has improved during my tenure.
I have refined the network coaching framework based on outcomes data.

activity.task: Design a network coaching impact tracking system. Implement for one term. Identify one network-wide coaching effectiveness gap. Design a refinement intervention. Submit to Director.
activity.output: Tracking system design + one-term data + intervention design.

cof-4_lead.reading: At the Lead level, HQ Subject Specialists shape coaching impact methodology beyond the Eduversal network — contributing to Cambridge community discussions on multi-school coaching impact tracking, presenting at international Cambridge conferences on cross-cultural coaching impact methodology, and mentoring specialists from other Cambridge regional networks. For example, a Biology specialist with 9 years' Eduversal experience presents at the Cambridge Schools Conference Asia on 'multi-school coaching impact tracking in Cambridge Indonesian-context partner-school networks: methodology and 4-year outcome data', writes a chapter for a Cambridge community publication on cross-cultural coaching impact, and mentors two early-career specialists from regional Cambridge networks through structured coaching impact system design cycles. The Indonesian-context coaching impact methodology — particularly the Go-action landing-rate tracking with year-on-year refinement — influences how Cambridge thinks about coaching impact evaluation across cultural contexts. This practice aligns with Cambridge International Teacher Standard 4.5 + 5.4.
keyTakeaways: Contribute to Cambridge community discussions on coaching impact methodology.
Publish or present on multi-school coaching impact tracking.
Mentor early-career specialists in coaching system design.
selfAssessment: I have presented at a regional Cambridge event on coaching impact methodology.
I contribute to Cambridge regional discussions on coaching impact tracking.
I mentor at least one early-career specialist in coaching system design.
I am cited within Cambridge community discussions on coaching impact methodology.
I have published or co-authored on multi-school coaching impact tracking.

activity.task: Submit a presentation proposal to a regional Cambridge event on multi-school coaching impact tracking. Mentor one early-career specialist through a coaching impact system design cycle.
activity.output: Submission record + mentoring cycle log.

tpd-1_awareness.reading: At the Awareness level, HQ Subject Specialists are beginning to design CPD workshops for partner-school teachers as deliberate professional events — with stated learning objectives, active participation, and tangible follow-up artefacts. A specialist at this stage can structure a 2-3 hour workshop with a clear learning intention, a balance of input and active practice, and a take-away artefact teachers can use the following week. For example, a newly appointed Biology specialist designs her first workshop on 'using diagrams as cognitive tools in IGCSE Biology' for partner-school teachers in Surabaya: she opens with 5 minutes of input, runs 90 minutes of active practice (teachers redesign one of their own diagrams), and closes with a takeaway artefact (a diagram-quality checklist they take to their next lesson). This practice aligns with CTS 3.5 + 4.4 + Eduversal Domain 6 (Collaboration / PD design). At this stage, the specialist designs workshops fluently but does not yet measurably track post-workshop practice change.
keyTakeaways: Design workshops with stated learning intentions, active participation, and takeaway artefacts.
Balance input (max 30%) with active practice (60%) with reflection (10%).
Provide a tangible takeaway teachers can use the following week.
selfAssessment: I structure workshops with clear learning intentions.
I balance input and active practice in my workshops.
I provide takeaway artefacts in every workshop.
I receive workshop feedback and refine designs based on it.
I can describe one workshop I designed and what teachers took away.

activity.task: Design a 2-hour partner-school CPD workshop in your subject. Include stated learning intentions, balanced input/practice/reflection, and a takeaway artefact. Deliver it. Collect feedback.
activity.output: Workshop design + delivery materials + feedback summary.

tpd-1_practitioner.reading: At the Practitioner level, HQ Subject Specialists consistently design and deliver workshops that produce measurable practice change. A specialist at this stage tracks post-workshop practice uptake (return for observation 3-6 weeks later), refines workshop design based on uptake data, and develops a portfolio of high-uptake workshop modules. For example, an English Language specialist designs and delivers 8 workshops across the year on different aspects of IGCSE First Language English (0500) teaching; she tracks practice uptake at 4-6 weeks for each workshop and discovers that 'concrete worked-example' workshops produce 70% uptake while 'theoretical principle' workshops produce 25% uptake. The data shapes her future design. This practice aligns with CTS 4.4 + 4.5. At this stage, the specialist designs effective workshops but does not yet contribute network-wide CPD strategy or shape workshop methodology beyond her own work.
keyTakeaways: Track post-workshop practice uptake — workshop satisfaction is not workshop impact.
Concrete, worked-example, takeaway-focused workshops produce far higher uptake than theoretical-principle workshops.
Build a portfolio of high-uptake workshop modules — over time it becomes a network resource.
selfAssessment: I track post-workshop practice uptake at 4-6 weeks.
I refine workshop design based on uptake data.
I have a portfolio of at least 5 high-uptake workshop modules.
Uptake rate of my workshops is above 50%.
I share workshop methodology with peer specialists.

activity.task: Track post-workshop practice uptake for three workshops you deliver this term. Compare uptake rates. Identify design factors that correlate with uptake. Refine your design approach.
activity.output: Uptake data + design factor analysis + refined design approach.

tpd-1_advanced.reading: At the Advanced level, HQ Subject Specialists systematically design network-wide CPD pathways — sequenced workshops that build coherent capability across a year. A specialist at this stage designs annual CPD pathways aligned to network priorities, sequences workshops so capability accrues, and tracks year-on-year network capability improvement. For example, a Mathematics specialist designs a year-long network CPD pathway for IGCSE Mathematics (0580) teachers: 8 workshops sequenced from foundational (mark-scheme literacy, command-word teaching) through pedagogical (questioning depth, formative assessment) to advanced (assessment design, moderation). After one year, network IGCSE Mathematics teachers measurably improve across all 8 workshop topics in formative assessment and observation evidence. This practice aligns with CTS 4.5 + Eduversal Network Standards. At this stage, the specialist designs network CPD pathways effectively but does not yet contribute Cambridge-facing publications on CPD pathway methodology.
keyTakeaways: Design CPD as a sequenced pathway, not a series of disconnected workshops.
Sequence builds capability — foundational → pedagogical → advanced.
Track year-on-year network capability improvement across the pathway.
selfAssessment: I design annual CPD pathways for my subject network.
My pathways are sequenced to build capability coherently.
I track year-on-year network capability improvement.
Network capability in my subject has improved during my tenure.
I have presented network CPD pathway findings to HQ Directors.

activity.task: Design a year-long CPD pathway for your subject network. Map workshops to network priorities. Sequence to build capability. Submit to HQ Director for resource approval.
activity.output: Year-long pathway design + Director approval record.

tpd-1_lead.reading: At the Lead level, HQ Subject Specialists shape network-wide CPD pathway methodology beyond the Eduversal network. A specialist at this stage contributes to Cambridge community discussions on department-level CPD design, presents at international Cambridge conferences on multi-subject CPD methodology, and mentors specialists from other Cambridge regional networks in pathway design. For example, an English Language specialist with 8 years' Eduversal experience presents at the Cambridge Schools Conference Asia on 'sequenced annual CPD pathways in Cambridge Indonesian-context partner-school networks: design methodology and 3-year outcome data', writes a chapter for a Cambridge community publication on cross-cultural CPD design, and mentors two early-career specialists from regional Cambridge networks (Singapore, Thailand) through structured 12-month CPD pathway design cycles. The Indonesian-context CPD pathway methodology influences how Cambridge thinks about teacher development across regional partner networks. This practice aligns with Cambridge International Teacher Standard 4.5 + 5.4. At this stage, the specialist shapes CPD methodology at the international Cambridge community level.
keyTakeaways: Contribute to Cambridge community discussions on CPD design.
Publish or present on network-wide CPD pathway methodology.
Mentor early-career specialists in CPD pathway design.
