tpd-2_awareness.reading: At the Awareness level, HQ Subject Specialists are beginning to teach model lessons and co-teach at partner schools. A specialist at this stage can plan and deliver a lesson in a partner-school classroom, recognises that modelling teaching is fundamentally different from describing teaching, and is beginning to follow up modelled lessons with co-planning conversations. For example, a Biology specialist visiting a partner school in Solo offers to teach a Year 11 IGCSE Biology (0610) lesson on photosynthesis — a topic the host teacher has flagged as challenging. The specialist plans the lesson with the host teacher, teaches it, and then co-debriefs. The host teacher sees specific routines (think-pair-share for predictions, structured analogies for the light reactions) she can adopt. This practice aligns with CTS 3.2 + 3.5 + Eduversal Domain 6. At this stage, the specialist models effectively but does not yet sustain modelling-co-teaching cycles.
keyTakeaways: Modelling teaching is fundamentally different from describing teaching — partner teachers see what is possible.
Co-plan with the host teacher before modelling — modelling without co-planning becomes performance, not teaching.
Always follow modelling with co-debrief — the conversation is where the learning lands.
selfAssessment: I have taught at least one model lesson at a partner school this term.
I co-plan with the host teacher before modelling.
I co-debrief with the host teacher after modelling.
I notice when partner teachers adopt routines I modelled.
I can describe one modelling cycle and its uptake.

activity.task: Conduct one full modelling cycle at a partner school: co-plan + model + co-debrief. Document what the host teacher took away and what they adopted in their next lessons.
activity.output: Cycle log + uptake observation.

tpd-2_practitioner.reading: At the Practitioner level, HQ Subject Specialists consistently sustain modelling-co-teaching cycles across multiple visits. A specialist at this stage runs structured co-teaching arrangements with partner teachers (specialist + teacher co-teach together, share planning, debrief jointly), tracks practice change across the cycle, and makes the modelling practice sustainable rather than one-off. For example, a Physics specialist sets up a co-teaching arrangement with a partner-school Year 12 AS Physics teacher in Bandung: across three visits over one semester, they co-plan and co-teach lessons together; gradually the partner teacher takes over more of the planning and delivery, the specialist becomes a critical friend in the room, and by visit 3 the routines are owned by the partner teacher. This practice aligns with CTS 3.2 + 3.5 + 4.4. At this stage, the specialist sustains co-teaching effectively but does not yet shape network-wide co-teaching methodology.
keyTakeaways: Sustain modelling-co-teaching across multiple visits — single sessions rarely transfer routines.
Gradually transfer ownership: specialist leads → co-leads → critical friend.
Track practice change across the cycle — that is the measurable outcome.
selfAssessment: I run structured co-teaching arrangements with at least one partner teacher per term.
I gradually transfer ownership across the cycle.
I track practice change across the cycle.
I can describe a co-teaching cycle and what the partner teacher owns at the end.
I share co-teaching methodology with peer specialists.

activity.task: Set up a 3-visit co-teaching arrangement with one partner teacher this semester. Document each visit. Track ownership transfer and practice change.
activity.output: Three co-teaching visit logs + ownership transfer analysis.

tpd-2_advanced.reading: At the Advanced level, HQ Subject Specialists shape network-wide co-teaching methodology. A specialist at this stage trains other specialists in modelling-co-teaching cycles, designs network co-teaching standards, and contributes to Eduversal CPD pathway design with co-teaching as a core component. For example, an English Language specialist trains 4 peer specialists in structured modelling-co-teaching cycles, contributes to the Eduversal network's CPD pathway with explicit co-teaching arrangements, and tracks network-wide co-teaching practice. After one year, all 7 active HQ specialists run structured co-teaching cycles, and partner-school teacher feedback on network CPD shifts from 'helpful but theoretical' to 'helpful and practical'. This practice aligns with CTS 4.4 + 4.5 + Eduversal Network Standards. At this stage, the specialist shapes network co-teaching methodology but does not yet contribute Cambridge-facing publications on co-teaching across cultural contexts.
keyTakeaways: Train peer specialists in modelling-co-teaching cycles — your network's co-teaching capacity scales through training.
Embed co-teaching in network CPD pathway design.
Track network-wide co-teaching practice.

For example, a Biology specialist with 8 years' Eduversal experience presents at a regional Cambridge event on 'sustained modelling-co-teaching cycles in Cambridge Indonesian-context partner-school networks', writes for Cambridge community channels on cross-cultural co-teaching insights, and mentors two early-career specialists from regional Cambridge networks (Thailand, Vietnam) through structured 6-month co-teaching cycle design programmes. tpd-3_awareness.reading: At the Awareness level, HQ Subject Specialists are beginning to understand structured one-to-one teacher coaching cycles — explicit, named, time-bounded coaching relationships with one partner teacher across a term — as different from informal 'I'll drop in when I can' support. A specialist at this stage can structure a coaching cycle (goal → action → observe → reflect → next step), recognises that coaching is a sustained relationship not a transactional intervention, and is beginning to commit to one structured cycle per term. For example, a Mathematics specialist commits to coaching a partner-school Year 9 Mathematics teacher in Bali across one term: they meet at the start of term to set a development goal (questioning depth), the specialist observes monthly, debriefs after each visit, and they meet at term end to reflect. This practice aligns with CTS 4.1 + 4.4 + Eduversal Domain 6 (coaching). At this stage, the specialist runs cycles but does not yet maintain multiple parallel cycles or develop coaching expertise across the network.
keyTakeaways: Structured coaching cycles are different from informal drop-in support.
A coaching cycle has explicit beginning, middle, and end — goal, action, reflection.
Commit to at least one structured cycle per term.
selfAssessment: I run at least one structured coaching cycle per term.
I can describe the goal, action steps, and reflection points of my current cycle.

activity.task: Set up one structured coaching cycle with a partner teacher this term. Document goal, monthly visits, debriefs, and end-of-term reflection.
activity.output: Coaching cycle log + reflection.

tpd-3_practitioner.reading: At the Practitioner level, HQ Subject Specialists consistently maintain multiple parallel coaching cycles — coaching 3-5 partner teachers concurrently across an academic year, with each cycle running for one full term. A specialist at this stage runs cycles fluently, tracks impact across multiple cycles using a teacher-by-teacher coaching impact log, and adjusts coaching approach based on what works for which teacher (different teachers have different progression rates, different stretch tolerances, different professional learning histories). For example, a Chemistry specialist runs 4 parallel coaching cycles across one year: 4 partner-school IGCSE Chemistry (0620) teachers, 4 different development goals (questioning depth, command-word teaching, formative assessment, mark-scheme literacy), 4 different progression rates (Rohan in Solo progresses fastest with regular co-planning support; Ibu Sari in Surabaya progresses through structured peer-observation cycles; Ibu Anjani needs more modelling). The differentiated coaching approach produces measurable outcome change in 3 of the 4 teachers within the year. This practice aligns with Cambridge International Teacher Standard 4.1 + 4.4.
keyTakeaways: Maintain multiple parallel coaching cycles — 3-5 concurrent is realistic and impactful.
Different teachers progress at different rates — adjust pace to the teacher, not the calendar.
Track impact across all cycles to surface what works for whom.
selfAssessment: I maintain 3-5 parallel coaching cycles concurrently.
I track impact across multiple cycles.
I adjust pace and approach to individual teachers.
I can describe coaching outcomes across at least 3 teachers I have coached.
Other specialists ask me about coaching methodology.

activity.task: Run 3 parallel coaching cycles across one term. Document each. Identify what works differently for each teacher.
activity.output: Three cycle logs + cross-cycle pattern analysis.

tpd-3_advanced.reading: At the Advanced level, HQ Subject Specialists train other observers in structured coaching cycle methodology and shape network coaching capacity. A specialist at this stage trains AH leaders in coaching cycles, develops network coaching standards, and contributes to network-wide coaching capacity development. For example, an English Language specialist trains 8 AH leaders (subject leaders + academic coordinators) in structured coaching cycle methodology over one year: she runs 4 training sessions, observes each leader's first cycle, provides follow-up coaching, and tracks network coaching capacity. After one year, network coaching capacity expands from 7 specialists to 15 trained coaches. This practice aligns with CTS 4.4 + 4.5 + Eduversal Network Standards. At this stage, the specialist shapes network coaching capacity but does not yet contribute Cambridge-facing publications on coaching methodology.
keyTakeaways: Train AH leaders in coaching methodology — your network's coaching capacity scales through training.
Provide follow-up coaching to early-career coaches in their first cycles.
Track network coaching capacity growth — outcomes prove the training.

tpd-4_awareness.reading: At the Awareness level, HQ Subject Specialists are beginning to design year-long CPD pathways for partner-school subject departments — sequenced workshops + coaching cycles + assessment evidence that build coherent capability across a year. A specialist at this stage can plan a department-level CPD pathway: who learns what, when, how it's evidenced, how leadership tracks it. For example, a Physics specialist designs a year-long CPD pathway for the Mathematics department at a partner school in Yogyakarta: 4 termly workshops (questioning depth → formative assessment → command-word teaching → moderation), monthly coaching observations for the department head, and assessment evidence at term end. This practice aligns with CTS 4.4 + 4.5 + Eduversal Domain 6 (PD design). At this stage, the specialist designs department pathways but does not yet contribute network-wide pathway methodology.
keyTakeaways: Department CPD pathways are sequenced — workshops + coaching + assessment evidence.
Department pathways need leadership engagement — track who's accountable for what.

tpd-4_practitioner.reading: At the Practitioner level, HQ Subject Specialists consistently design and deliver department-level CPD pathways with measurable capability outcomes. A specialist at this stage runs multiple department pathways across the network, tracks year-on-year department capability improvement, and refines pathway design based on outcomes. For example, a Biology specialist runs department CPD pathways at 4 partner schools simultaneously, tracks year-on-year capability improvement across all 4 departments, and discovers that pathways with monthly leader engagement produce 40% more uptake than pathways with termly leader engagement. The data shapes her future design. This practice aligns with CTS 4.4 + 4.5. At this stage, the specialist runs department pathways effectively but does not yet contribute network-wide pathway design.
keyTakeaways: Run multiple department pathways simultaneously — 3-5 concurrent is realistic.
Track year-on-year capability improvement across pathways.
Monthly leader engagement produces far higher uptake than termly engagement.
